experiment: DISC_CANCER_2_v1 -- NSCLC Perturbation Loop: Which node removal collapses structural organization most?
description: Second iteration of the NSCLC discovery loop. Tests which node removal from DISC_CANCER_1 candidates (GRB2, ERK1, MYC) causes the largest drop in r(physical_interaction, coexpression_cancer) from the baseline of r=0.7145. This is a structural weak point test: the node whose removal most disrupts the cross-layer coupling IS the structural bottleneck. Pre-registered prediction: removing MYC will cause the largest r drop (delta < -0.08) because MYC is the only node that is simultaneously a top-3 hub in coexpression_cancer (rank #2) and has zero drug coverage -- it is the biological hub most disconnected from therapeutic coverage. GRB2 removal will be second (physical hub, structural bridge to KRAS). ERK1 removal will have minimal impact (linear relay, easily bypassed via mTOR->MYC path). This experiment runs on the MYC-removed graph (examples/nsclc_no_myc.json), generated by the perturbation loop script. The perturbation loop will also generate and test GRB2-removed and ERK1-removed graphs for comparison. Dataset: examples/nsclc_no_myc.json (16 nodes minus MYC = 15 nodes). Baseline r = 0.7145 from DISC_CANCER_1_v1.

hypotheses.statement: After removing MYC from the NSCLC network, r(physical_interaction, coexpression_cancer) drops below the baseline (baseline = 0.7145 from DISC_CANCER_1). Pre-registered threshold: r < 0.65 after MYC removal. MYC contributes to d1-d2 correlation because it is a physical interaction hub receiving from ERK1 and mTOR (d1) AND the #2 coexpression hub in TCGA data (d2). Removing MYC: (a) removes the convergence point of both MAPK and PI3K pathways in the physical network, (b) removes 3 coexpression edges (MYC-mTOR, MYC-PI3K, MDM2-MYC) that contribute to d2 density. Expected: r drops to ~0.55-0.65. If CONFIRMED (r still >= 0.65): MYC removal is surprisingly resilient -- the network has structural redundancy that compensates. If DENIED (r < 0.65): MYC removal disrupts cross-layer coupling -- MYC is the structural weak point whose removal most degrades the cancer network's organization.

After MYC removal, EGFR remains the top hub in physical_interaction. MYC removal does not affect EGFR's degree in d1 (EGFR had no edges to/from MYC). EGFR degree in d1 was 6 and remains 6 after MYC removal. If CONFIRMED: the top physical hub is stable -- drug development correctly identified EGFR as the primary target. If DENIED (another protein takes rank #1): an unexpected structural reorganization has occurred, meaning the network structure is more sensitive to MYC than anticipated.

After MYC removal, AKT1 or PI3K becomes the top betweenness hub, displacing EGFR. Reasoning: MYC removal disconnects the path from ERK1 to TP53 (ERK1->MYC->TP53 no longer exists). The remaining paths to TP53 go through MDM2->TP53 only. This changes betweenness distribution: nodes on the AKT1->MDM2->TP53 path (AKT1, MDM2) may accumulate more betweenness as they become the sole bottleneck to TP53. If CONFIRMED: betweenness shifts from EGFR to PI3K/AKT1 pathway after MYC removal -- meaning PI3K pathway becomes the new structural bottleneck. This would be a direct prescription: if MYC is the structural weak point, the SECOND priority target is the PI3K-AKT1 arm. If DENIED (EGFR still top betweenness): EGFR maintains structural dominance regardless of MYC presence.
